content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 22, 1992


Paul H. Chamberlain1
CHAMBERLAIN, CHAMBERLAIN & BALDO
P. O. BOX 32
Auburn, CA  95604-0032

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. I-91-396

Dear Mr. Chamberlain:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the duties and responsibilities of Wild West Graphics
and Communications, Edward Miller, and Lolly Kupec under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").2  We are unable to determine, from the facts provided, to
what degree Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec will participate in
governmental decisions in that term is defined in the Act.
Therefore, we provide you with a general discussion of the
applicable provisions of the Act pursuant to the provisions of
Regulation 18329.3


QUESTIONS

1.  Under the terms of the proposed contract between the
Truckee City Public Utility District and Wild West Graphics and
Communications, are Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec "consultants"
for purposes of the Act?


_______________

1 This letter is addressed to you pursuant to our conversation of
August 29, 1991, at which time you advised me that although you
had asked Mr. Baldo, of your firm, to sign your request for advice
in your absence, you continue to handle this matter.

2 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
Regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

3 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 2


2. Are Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec disqualified from
participating in decisions affecting the North Tahoe Fine Arts
Council?

3. Under the provisions of the Act, may Edward Miller and
Lolly Kupec provide services to the Tahoe City Public Utility
District regarding activities, programs, or plans which are not
related to the performing arts?

CONCLUSIONS

1. We are unable to determine from the contract you have
provided for our review whether Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec
would be consultants for purposes of the Act. However, if their
work for the Tahoe City Public Utility District falls within the
definition of consultant contained in Regulation 18700, as
discussed below, then they would be consultants and subject to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.

2. If Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec meet the definition of
consultant, then they would be disqualified from participating in
decisions which would foreseeably and materially affect Wild West
Graphics and Communications or clients of the business, including
the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council, as discussed below.

3. The provisions of the Act would not prohibit Edward
Miller and Lolly Kupec from providing services to the Tahoe City
Public Utility District regarding activities, programs, or plans
which are not related to the performing arts unless such
activities, programs, or plans would have a foreseeable and
material financial effect on Wild West Graphics and Communications
or its clients.

FACTS

The Tahoe City Public Utility District and the North Tahoe
Public Utility District are utility districts organized under
California Public Utilities Code Sections 15500 et seq. The Tahoe
City Public Utility District extends from Emerald Bay on the west
shore of Lake Tahoe and northerly along the west shore of Lake
Tahoe terminating at the summit of Dollar Hill which lies north of
the unincorporated town known as Tahoe City. The North Tahoe
Public Utility District extends from the Dollar Hill area along
the west and north shores of Lake Tahoe ending at the California/
Nevada State Line. Both utility districts provide water and sewer
services within their respective territories and additionally,
each provides recreational services and facilities.

In recent years, the boards of directors of the respective
districts, in conjunction with a representative of the Placer
County Board of Supervisors representing the north lake Tahoe
area, have expressed interest in undertaking initial studies to
determine the feasibility and to consider the initial planning
stages for regional recreation and community facilities projects

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 3


to benefit their respective jurisdictions. One of the projects
contemplated is a regional recreation complex.

In April of 1991, the Tahoe City Public Utility District was
designated as the lead agency for the cooperative planning process
for the proposed regional recreation and community facilities
project, including control of the planning process. As an initial step in implementing these projects, a joint regional
recreation committee was formed. This committee is
comprised of representatives of each of the utility districts as
well as representatives of Placer County. The Tahoe Transient
Occupancy Tax Advisory Committee, an advisory agency to the Board
of Supervisors of Placer County, is also represented in the joint
regional recreation committee. The committee is not subject to a
formal written agreement. From its membership, the joint regional
recreation committee has appointed a steering committee, also
known as the planning team, to act as an advisory committee to the
Tahoe City Public Utility District, the lead agency in the
project.

As an initial stage in preparation for the project, the Tahoe
City Public Utility District prepared and processed requests
for proposals for design and architectural services. Separate
requests for proposals were prepared and processed for a public
participation component. Responses to the requests for proposals
were screened by a committee which graded the proponents and made
recommendations for selection to the board of directors of the
Tahoe City Public Utility District. The contract for
architectural services was granted to Sagan Design. The screening
committee recommended contracting with Wild West Graphics and
Communications to provide public participation services as "public
involvement coordinator."

You have enclosed for our consideration a letter dated
August 16, 1991, from Wild West Graphics and Communications to
David Antonucci of the Tahoe City Public Utility District. This
letter is marked Exhibit "A" and is attached to a draft contract
between the Tahoe City Public Utility District and Wild West
Graphics and Communications. The proposed contract incorporates
this letter and refers to the letter for a determination of the
services to be provided by Wild West Graphics and Communications.
As listed in this letter, the services to be provided are as
follows:

1. Attend meetings for the Planning Team.
Prepare visual aids, exhibits and outlines as
directed by the planning team.

2. Prepare draft press releases, arrange
media interviews and coordinate media.

3. Graphic design, production and placement
of newspaper advertising.

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 4


     4. Provide impartial, neutral and encouraging
environments for public input for three initial
town meetings, five follow-up meetings and eighteen
focused committee meetings.

     5. Conduct random samplings public opinion
survey and report results.

     6. Prepare reports of results of public
meetings.

     7. Graphic design and production of camera
ready artwork for public information flyer.

     If granted the contract, representatives of Wild West
Graphics and Communications, as the public involvement coordinator
for the regional recreation complex master plan, would attend
meetings of the planning team, prepare media releases, conduct
random sampling public opinion surveys, coordinate public meetings,
and prepare reports of the public
meetings for submission to the planning team.

Wild West Graphics and Communications is owned and operated
by Edward Miller and his spouse, Lolly Kupec.⁴ Until recently,
Edward Miller was also an employee active as an executive
officer for the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council, a nonprofit
corporation serving the north Lake Tahoe areas of Placer and
Nevada counties as well as portions of the State of Nevada. This
nonprofit corporation is dedicated to the improvement of the arts
in the north Lake Tahoe area. This nonprofit corporation is also
a client of Wild West Graphics and Communications. Lolly Kupec is
the volunteer president emeritus of the board of directors of the
North Tahoe Fine Arts Council.

     You state in your request for advice that Wild West Graphics
and Communications will have no authority to make decisions for
the contracting agency, the Tahoe City Public Utility District, or
for the joint regional recreation committee. As legal counsel for
the Tahoe City Public Utility District, you seek our advice to
determine whether Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec would be precluded from
discharging their obligations under the proposed contract due to
their involvement with the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council.


___________

⁴ These facts contain information you provided to me during the
course of our telephone conversation on August 29, 1991, and
information provided by Mr. Miller on October 18, 1991. The
Commission is not a finder of fact. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71, 77.) Accordingly, our discussion is limited to the facts
as stated in this letter.

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 5


ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

Public Official

Section 82048 defines a "public official" as "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency....." (Emphasis added.)

Regulation 18700 defines a consultant as follows:

"Consultant" shall include any natural person
who provides, under contract, information, advice,
recommendation or counsel to a state or local
government agency, provided, however, that
"consultant" shall not include a person who:

(A) Conducts research and arrives at
conclusions with respect to his or her rendition of
information, advice, recommendation or counsel
independent of the control and direction of the
agency or of any agency official, other than normal
contract monitoring; and

(B) Possesses no authority with respect to
any agency decision beyond the rendition of
information, advice, recommendation or counsel.

Regulation 18700(a)(2).

Regulation 18700(a)(2) defines a "consultant" as a "natural
person." Therefore, Wild West Graphics and Communications would
not be a consultant within the meaning of the Act. It is the
personnel who perform the work who may be deemed consultants.

In In re Maloney (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69, with respect to a
contract county surveyor-engineer, the Commission stated:

Our regulation defining the term "consultant" ...
excludes a person who does no more than provide
advice, information, recommendation or counsel to
an agency and whose advice is provided independent
of the agency's control or discretion. 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18700(a)(2). The preparation of
surveys and engineering studies would appear to
fall within this exclusion. When performing these
services, the county surveyor-engineer is not
involved in any official decision making. He is
merely carrying out the terms of a contract just as
any vendor of goods or services to the county
might. He is not subject to the control or
discretion of the county when he performs his work.

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 6


but is governed only by the provisions of his
contract.

In re Maloney, supra, at 71
(emphasis added).

Accordingly, if Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec provide services to
the Tahoe City Public Utility District independent of the control
and direction of the district or its agents, and governed only by
the provisions of the contract, they will not be deemed to be
consultants within the meaning of the Act. (Coomes Advice Letter,
No. I-91-306.)

We are unable to determine from the list of duties you have
submitted for our consideration the degree of autonomy that Wild
West Graphics and Communications will be granted nor to whom
Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec will report. However, to the extent that
Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec act in accordance with the language in
Regulation 18700(a)(2)(A) and (B), Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec will
not be deemed consultants within the meaning of the Act.

Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision.

A public official makes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency. (Regulation 18700(b). Additionally, a public
official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review5 with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:


5 If public agency officials or their agents conduct significant
intervening substantive review of the work prepared by a
contractor, the contractor may not be participating in
governmental decisions for purposes of the Act. Regulation
18700(c)(2) contemplates that significant intervening
substantive review will be conducted by agency officials or their
agents. Thus, a significant intervening substantive review by
persons who are not agents of the district may nevertheless cause
a public official's activities to constitute "participating in the
making of a governmental decision" within the meaning of the Act.
(Coomes, supra.)

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 7


                    (A)  Conducting research or making any
            investigation which requires the exercise of
            judgment on the part of the official or
            designated employee and the purpose of which
            is to influence the decision; or

                    (B)  Preparing or presenting any report,
            analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
            which requires the exercise of judgment on the
            part of the official or designated employee
            and the purpose of which is to influence the
            decision.

                                   Regulation 18700(c).

     With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency.  Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)

     To the extent that Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec avoid acting as
described in Regulation 18700(c), they will not be deemed to have
participated in the making of a governmental decision.  However,
if you determine that Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec are consultants,
they must disqualify themselves from participating in making
decisions if it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will
have a material financial effect on Wild West Graphics and
Communications, and on any client thereof which has a source
of income to the business of $250 or more in the 12 months
preceding the governmental decision, as discussed below.6


_____________________
     6  Please note that the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council will not be
deemed a source of income as a former employer of Mr. Miller's for
purposes of Section 87103(c) if all income was received by or
accrued to Mr. Miller prior to the time he contracted with the
North Tahoe Public Utility District, the income was received in
the normal course of the previous employment, and Mr. Miller had
no expectation of further employment with the North Tahoe Fine
Arts Council at the time he contracted with the North Tahoe Public
Utility District. (Regulation 18704.) However, if Mr. Miller was
not an employee of the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council but received
income from this organization as a consultant, then Regulation
18704 would not apply.  In addition, because the North Tahoe Fine
Arts Council does business with Wild West Graphics and
Communications, it is therefore a source of income to Mr. Miller
and Ms. Kupec.

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 8


Financial Interests

    An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a
member of the official's immediate family,7 or on:

    (a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

    (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

    (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

    (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

                        * * *

    For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

                        Section 87103(c).

    Wild West Graphics and Communications is a business entity
wholly owned by Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec. Presumably, they have
an investment in this business entity which is greater than


_________________________
7   An official's "immediate family" are his or her spouse and
dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 9


$1,000.8 Consequently, any person or business that has made a
payment of $250 or more within the past 12 months to Wild West
Graphics and Communications is a source of income to Mr. Miller
and Ms. Kupec. Thus, they may participate in any governmental
decisions which will foreseeably and materially affect these
sources or income in a manner which is distinguishable from the
effect of the decisions on the public generally. (Goldyey Advice
Letter, No. I-91-462.) Additionally, Wild West Graphics and
Communications is a source of income in excess of $250 in the
preceding twelve months to Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec. Thus, they
may not participate in governmental decisions which will
foreseeably and materially affect this business entity.

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Develop. Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 317; In re Thorner (1975)
5 FPPC Ops. 133.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 323.)
It there is a substantial likelihood that any decision made or
participated in by Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec will have a financial
effect on their economic interests, as discussed below,
foreseeability is met.

Materiality

Even if it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will
have a financial effect on an official's economic interest, the
effect must be "material" in order to trigger disqualification.
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining whether
an official's economic interest in a decision is materially
affected by a decision. If the official's economic interest is
directly involved in the decision, Regulation 13702.1 provides the
appropriate standard for assessing materiality. However, when the
official's economic interests will be affected indirectly by a
decision, the appropriate standards for determining materiality
are those of Regulations 13702.2 through 13702.6. These
regulations are discussed below.

1. The North Tahoe Fine Arts Council

Mr. Miller's employment relationship with the North Tahoe
Fine Arts Council is not a financial interest if his employment

8
Section 82030 provides that the income of an individual
includes a pro-rata share of any income of any business entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly
or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater.

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 10


with this organization has terminated according to the terms of
Regulation 18704, discussed above. However, because Mr. Miller
has advised us that the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council, a nonprofit
corporation, is a client of Wild West Graphics and Communications,
it is thus a source of income to Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec.
Regulation 18702.1 provides that the financial effect of a
decision is material as to a public official of $250 or more in the preceding 12 months if the source of income
is directly involved in the decision before the official's agency
or there is a nexus between the income which the official
receives income and the governmental decision.

A source of income to a public official is directly involved
in a decision before an official's agency when that person or
entity, either personally or by an agent:

     (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;

     (2) Is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's agency.

     (3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.

                                    Regulation 18702.1(b).

Accordingly, if you determine that Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec are
consultants, the nonprofit corporation, as a source of income to
Wild West Graphics and Communications, is a potentially
disqualifying economic interest. Thus, Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec
may not participate in governmental decisions in which the
nonprofit corporation is directly involved.

Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec are also required to disqualify
themselves if the nonprofit corporation, as a client of their
business, were affected indirectly by decisions of the Tahoe City
Public Utility District in which Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec
participated as consultants. This would be the case, for example,
if a decision pending before the Tahoe City Public Utility
District would indirectly affect the gross annual receipts,
expenses or assets of the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council.

When a nonprofit entity is indirectly affected by a decision,
the standard for determining materiality is that of Regulation
18702.5. This regulation sets forth standards based upon the
gross annual receipts of the organization. For example, if the

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 11


nonprofit corporation has gross annual receipts of $100,000 or
less,9 the effect of a decision will be material if:

     (1)  The decision will result in an increase
or decrease of the entity's gross annual receipts
for a fiscal year in the amount of $10,000 or more.

     (2)  The decision will cause the entity to
incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or
eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in
the amount of $2,500 or more.

     (3)  The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the entity's assets or
liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more.

                    Regulation 18702.5(f).

     Therefore, if you determine that it is reasonably foreseeable
that a decision before the Tahoe City Public Utility District will
indirectly affect the nonprofit corporation which is a source of
income to Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec, their disqualification is
required if the decision will affect the nonprofit corporation in
the amounts indicated in Regulation 18702.5.

2.  Wild West Graphics and Communications

     Wild West Graphics and Communications is both a source of
income to Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec in excess of $250 in a twelve
month period and an investment presumably in excess of $1,000.
Consequently, Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec must disqualify themselves
from participating in governmental decisions which would
foreseeably and materially affect Wild West Graphics and
Communications.  Because making of a governmental decision does not include negotiating the terms and
conditions of an individual's compensation or employment or
contract negotiations (Regulation 18700(d)(1)), Mr. Miller and
Ms. Kupec are not precluded from negotiating the terms and
conditions of their contract with the Tahoe City Public Utility
District.  However, Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec would be disqualified
from participating in governmental decisions, as consultants to
the Tahoe City Public Utility District, which would foreseeably
and materially affect Wild West Graphics and Communications.  This
would be the case, for example, if Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec, as
consultants for the District, were to engage in activities which
would result in new business opportunities for Wild West Graphics
and Communications.


9 Of course, if the nonprofit corporation's gross annual receipts
were greater than $100,000, other provisions of Regulation 18702.5
would apply.

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 12


     When a business entity such as Wild West Graphics and
Communications is indirectly affected by a governmental decision,
the appropriate standard for determining materiality is that of
Regulation 13702.2.  Whether the direct effect of a decision is
material depends on the financial size of the business entity.
Regulation 13702.2 provides different thresholds of materiality
for:

     1.  Business entities listed on the New York Stock Exchange,
the American Stock Exchange, or business entities on the
Fortune Magazine Directory of the 500 largest U.S. industrial
corporations or the 500 largest U.S. nonindustrial corporations.
(Regulation 13702.2(a) and (d));

     2.  Business entities listed on the National Association of
Securities Dealers National Market List or any business entity
with net tangible assets of at least $18,000,000 and pre-tax
income for the last fiscal year of at least $2,500,000.
(Regulation 13702.2(b) and (e)); and

     3.  Business entities not fitting the requirements of (a) or
(b) but which are listed on the Pacific Stock Exchange or qualify
for public sale in California and are listed on the Eligible
Securities List maintained by the California Department of
Corporations.  Or, business entities with net tangible assets of
at least $4,000,000, and with pre-tax income for the last fiscal
year of at least $750,000, with net income from that period of at
least $400,000.  (Regulation 13702.2(c) and (f).)

     For any business entity not covered in one of these
categories, Regulation 13702.2(g) governs.  For a relatively small
business entity such as Wild West Graphics and Communications,
Regulation 13702.2(g) provides that the effect of a decision is
material where:

          (1)  The decision will result in an increase
     or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
     of $10,000 or more; or

          (2)  The decision will result in the business
     entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
     reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
     fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

          (3)  The decision will result in the increase
     or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
     of $10,000 or more.

     Thus, if subdivision (g) is the appropriate standard for
determining the material financial effect of a decision on Wild
West Graphics and Communications, Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec may not
participate in a governmental decision that could foreseeably
increase or decrease the gross revenues, assets or liabilities of
the business entity by $10,000 or more, or increase or decrease

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 13


expenses by $2,500. Obviously, if subdivision (g) is not
applicable, the appropriate subdivision in Regulation 18702.2
should be used to determine whether the effect of a decision is
material.

     3.  Clients of Wild West Graphics and Communications

     Clients of Wild West Graphics and Communications who have
been sources of income to this business entity of $250 or more in
the preceding twelve months are among the economic interests of
Mr. Miller and Ms. Rupec. (Kolodney, supra.) The appropriate
standard for determining materiality with regards to an individual
who is a source of income to an official is that of Regulation
18702.6 which states as follows:

     The effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official if any of the following applies:

          (a) The decision will affect the individual's
     income, investments, or other tangible or
     intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
     property) by $1,000 or more; or

          (b) The decision will affect the individual's
     real property interest in a manner that is
     considered material under Section 18702.3 or
     Section 18702.4.

     We are enclosing copies of Regulations 18702.3 and 18702.4 to
assist you in determining whether clients of Wild West Graphics
and Communications will be affected by the pending decisions in
such a manner as to result in a disqualifying conflict of interest
for Mr. Miller and Ms. Rupec.

Public generally

     Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all members of
the public in the same manner, disqualification may be required
unless the effect of the decision on the public official's
financial interests is the same as the effect on a significant
segment of the public. (Regulation 18703.) For purposes of our

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 14


discussion, the public are all the residents within the
jurisdiction of the Tahoe City Public Utility District.10 (In re
Logan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1; In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77;
Jorgensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-017.)

Accordingly, in the case of the nonprofit corporation which
is a source of income to Wild West Graphics and Communications,
the disqualification of Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec is required only
if the nonprofit corporation will be singularly affected in a
manner which is not similar to the effect of decisions of the
City Public Utility District on the general public. For
example, a decision to increase the charges for water usage would
presumably affect all members of the public in the jurisdiction.
In such an instance, the disqualification of Mr. Miller or
Ms. Kupec would not be required. The same analysis is applicable
to the economic interests of Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec in Wild West
Graphics and Communications and its clients.

Finally, you have asked whether Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec may
provide services to the Tahoe City Public Utility District
regarding activities, programs, or plans which are not related to
the performing arts. The Act does not contain any provisions
which would limit the ability of Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec to
provide services to prospective employers. The Act only prohibits
a public official from making, participating or attempting to
influence only those decisions in which the official knows or has
reason to know that he or she has a financial interest. (Section
37100.) Accordingly, if they are deemed to be consultants,
Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec must disqualify themselves from
participating in governmental decisions which will have a
foreseeable and material financial effect on their economic
interests. The same analysis discussed above, would be
required to make this determination. Absent such a financial
effect, the Act imposes no limits on their activities.


10     However, when public officials make decisions regarding areas
which are located outside the political boundaries of the
district, conflict-of-interest issues may arise due to expanded
jurisdiction. For purposes of the Act, the jurisdiction of a
public agency encompasses the region, county, city, district or
other geographical area in which it has jurisdiction. (Section
82025.) Therefore, because the Tahoe City Public Utility District
is the lead agency for this planning process, if you determine
that Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec are public officials, they must
disqualify themselves from participating in decisions which will
affect their economic interests even when such economic interests
are situated outside the jurisdiction of the Tahoe City Public
Utility District so long as such interests are located in the
expanded jurisdiction encompassed by the project. (Letter to Tri-
Cities Municipal Water District dated March 14, 1991.)

Our File No. I-91-396
Page 15


We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.11

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Blanca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BNB:bb

Enclosures: Regulations 18702.3, 18702.4


11 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question:
1. Under the terms of the proposed contract between the Tahoe City Public Utility District and Wild West Graphics and Communications, are Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec "consultants" for purposes of the Act?

---

¹ This letter is addressed to you pursuant to our conversation of August 29, 1991, at which time you advised me that although you had asked Mr. Baldo, of your firm, to sign your request for advice in your absence, you continue to handle this matter.

², et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

2. Are Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec disqualified from participating in decisions affecting the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council?

3. Under the provisions of the Act, may Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec provide services to the Tahoe City Public Utility District regarding activities, programs, or plans which are not related to the performing arts?
sections.conclusion:
1. We are unable to determine from the contract you have provided for our review whether Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec would be consultants for purposes of the Act. However, if their work for the Tahoe City Public Utility District falls within the definition of consultant contained in Regulation 18700, as discussed below, then they would be consultants and subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.

2. If Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec meet the definition of consultant, then they would be disqualified from participating in decisions which would foreseeably and materially affect Wild West Graphics and Communications or clients of the business, including the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council, as discussed below.

3. The provisions of the Act would not prohibit Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec from providing services to the Tahoe City Public Utility District regarding activities, programs, or plans which are not related to the performing arts unless such activities, programs, or plans would have a foreseeable and material financial effect on Wild West Graphics and Communications or its clients.
sections.facts:
The Tahoe City Public Utility District and the North Tahoe Public Utility District are utility districts organized under California Public Utilities Code Sections 15500 et seq. The Tahoe City Public Utility District extends from Emerald Bay on the west shore of Lake Tahoe and northerly along the west shore of Lake Tahoe terminating at the summit of Dollar Hill which lies north of the unincorporated town known as Tahoe City. The North Tahoe Public Utility District extends from the Dollar Hill area along the west and north shores of Lake Tahoe ending at the California/Nevada State Line. Both utility districts provide water and sewer services within their respective territories and additionally, each provides recreational services and facilities.

In recent years, the boards of directors of the respective districts, in conjunction with a representative of the Placer County Board of Supervisors representing the north Lake Tahoe area, have expressed interest in undertaking initial studies to determine the feasibility and to consider the initial planning stages for regional recreation and community facilities projects.

to benefit their respective jurisdictions. One of the projects contemplated is a regional recreation complex.

In April of 1991, the Tahoe City Public Utility District was designated as the lead agency for the cooperative planning process for the proposed regional recreation and community facilities project, including control and direction of the planning process. As an initial step in implementing these projects, a joint regional recreation committee was formed. This committee is comprised of representatives from each of the utility districts as well as representatives of Placer County. The Tahoe Transient Occupancy Tax Advisory Committee, an advisory agency to the Board of Supervisors of Placer County, is also represented in the joint regional recreation committee. The committee is not subject to a formal written agreement. From its membership, the joint regional recreation committee has appointed a steering committee, also known as the planning team, to act as an advisory committee to the Tahoe City Public Utility District, the lead agency in the project.

As an initial stage in preparation for the project, the Tahoe City Public Utility District prepared and processed requests for proposals for design and architectural services. Separate requests for proposals were prepared and processed for a public participation component. Responses to the requests for proposals were screened by a committee which graded the proponents and made recommendations for selection to the board of directors of the Tahoe City Public Utility District. The contract for architectural services was granted to Sagan Design. The screening committee recommended contracting with Wild West Graphics and Communications to provide public participation services as "public involvement coordinator."

You have enclosed for our consideration a letter dated August 16, 1991, from Wild West Graphics and Communications to David Antonucci of the Tahoe City Public Utility District. This letter is marked Exhibit "A" and is attached to a draft contract between the Tahoe City Public Utility District and Wild West Graphics and Communications. The proposed contract incorporates this letter and refers to the letter for a determination of the services to be provided by Wild West Graphics and Communications. As listed in this letter, the services to be provided are as follows:

1. Attend meetings for the Planning Team. Prepare visual aids, exhibits and outlines as directed by the planning team.

2. Prepare draft press releases, arrange media interviews and coordinate media.

3. Graphic design, production and placement of newspaper advertising.

4. Provide impartial, neutral and encouraging environments for public input for three initial town meetings, five follow-up meetings and eighteen focused committee meetings.

5. Conduct random samplings public opinion survey and report results.

6. Prepare reports of results of public meetings.

7. Graphic design and production of camera ready artwork for public information flyer.

If granted the contract, representatives of Wild West Graphics and Communications, as the public involvement coordinator for the regional recreation complex master plan, would attend meetings of the planning team, prepare media releases, conduct random sampling public opinion surveys and report on the results, coordinate public meetings, and prepare reports of the public meetings for submission to the planning team.

Wild West Graphics and Communications is owned and operated by Edward Miller and his spouse, Lolly Kupec.4 Until recently, Edward Miller was also an employee acting as chief executive officer for the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council, a nonprofit corporation serving the north Lake Tahoe areas of Placer and Nevada Counties as well as portions of the State of Nevada. This nonprofit corporation is dedicated to the improvement of the arts in the north Lake Tahoe area. This nonprofit corporation is also a client of Wild West Graphics and Communications. Lolly Kupec is the volunteer president emeritus of the board of directors of the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council.

You state in your request for advice that Wild West Graphics and Communications will have no authority to make decisions for the contracting agency, the Tahoe City Public Utility District, or for the joint regional recreation committee. As legal counsel for the Tahoe City Public Utility District, you seek our advice to determine whether Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec would be precluded from discharging their obligations under the proposed contract due to their involvement with the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council.

4 These facts contain information you provided to me during the course of our telephone conversation on August 29, 1991, and information provided by Mr. Miller on October 18, 1991. The Commission is not a finder of fact. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.) Accordingly, our discussion is limited to the facts as stated in this letter.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in, or using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know they have a financial interest.

Public Official

Section 82048 defines a "public official" as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency...." (Emphasis added.)

Regulation 18700 defines a consultant as follows:

"Consultant" shall include any natural person who provides, under contract, information, advice, recommendation or counsel to a state or local government agency, provided, however, that "consultant" shall not include a person who:

(A) Conducts research and arrives at conclusions with respect to his or her rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel independent of the control and direction of the agency or of any agency official, other than normal contract monitoring; and

(B) Possesses no authority with respect to any agency decision beyond the rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel.

Regulation 18700(a)(2).

Regulation 18700(a)(2) defines a "consultant" as a "natural person." Therefore, Wild West Graphics and Communications would not be a consultant within the meaning of the Act. It is the personnel who perform the work who may be deemed consultants.

In In re Maloney (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69, with respect to a contract county surveyor-engineer, the Commission stated:

Our regulation defining the term "consultant" ... excludes a person who does no more than provide advice, information, recommendation or counsel to an agency and whose advice is provided independent of the agency's control or discretion. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2). The preparation of surveys and engineering studies would appear to fall within this exclusion. When performing these services, the county surveyor-engineer is not involved in any official decision making. He is merely carrying out the terms of a contract just as any vendor of goods or services to the county might. He is not subject to the control or discretion of the county when he performs his work.

but is governed only by the provisions of his contract.

In re Maloney, supra, at 71 (emphasis added).

Accordingly, if Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec provide services to the Tahoe City Public Utility District independent of the control and direction of the district or its agents, and governed only by the provisions of the contract, they will not be deemed to be consultants within the meaning of the Act. (Coomes Advice Letter, No. I-91-306.)

We are unable to determine from the list of duties you have submitted for our consideration the degree of autonomy that Wild West Graphics and Communications will be granted nor to whom Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec will report. However, to the extent that Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec act in accordance with the language in Regulation 18700(a)(2)(A) and (B), Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec will not be deemed consultants within the meaning of the Act.

Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a Governmental Decision.

A public official makes a governmental decision or participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of the agency. (Regulation 18700(b). Additionally, a public official participates in a governmental decision when, acting within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review5 with a governmental entity or private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker, either directly or without significant intervening substantive review, by:

5 If public agency officials or their agents conduct significant intervening substantive review of the work prepared by a contractor, the contractor may not be participating in governmental decisions for purposes of the Act. Regulation 18700(c)(2) contemplates that such significant intervening substantive review will be conducted by agency officials or their agents. Thus, a significant intervening substantive review by persons who are not agents of the district may nevertheless cause a public official's activities to constitute "participating in the making of a governmental decision" within the meaning of the Act. (Coomes, supra.)

(A) Conducting research or making any investigation which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision; or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision.

Regulation 18700(c).

With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)

To the extent that Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec avoid acting as described in Regulation 18700(c), they will not be deemed to have participated in the making of a governmental decision. However, if you determine that Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec are consultants, they must disqualify themselves from participating in making decisions if it is reasonably foreseeable that such decisions will have a material financial effect on Wild West Graphics and Communications, and on any client thereof which has been a source of income to the business of $250 or more in the 12 months preceding the governmental decision, as discussed below.6

6 Please note that the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council will not be deemed a source of income as a former employer of Mr. Miller's for purposes of Section 87103(c) if all income was received by or accrued to Mr. Miller prior to the time he contracted with the North Tahoe Public Utility District, the income was received in the normal course of the previous employment, and Mr. Miller had no expectation of further employment with the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council at the time he contracted with the North Tahoe Public Utility District. (Regulation 18704.) However, if Mr. Miller was not an employee of the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council but received income from this organization as a consultant, then Regulation 18704 would not apply. In addition, because the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council does business with Wild West Graphics and Communications, it is therefore a source of income to Mr. Miller and Ms. Kupec.

Financial Interests

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a member of the official's immediate family,7 or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

---

¹ This letter is addressed to you pursuant to our conversation of August 29, 1991, at which time you advised me that although you had asked Mr. Baldo, of your firm, to sign your request for advice in your absence, you continue to handle this matter.

², et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

2. Are Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec disqualified from participating in decisions affecting the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council?

3. Under the provisions of the Act, may Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec provide services to the Tahoe City Public Utility District regarding activities, programs, or plans which are not related to the performing arts?

CONCLUSION: 1. We are unable to determine from the contract you have provided for our review whether Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec would be consultants for purposes of the Act. However, if their work for the Tahoe City Public Utility District falls within the definition of consultant contained in Regulation 18700, as discussed below, then they would be consultants and subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.

2. If Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec meet the definition of consultant, then they would be disqualified from participating in decisions which would foreseeably and materially affect Wild West Graphics and Communications or clients of the business, including the North Tahoe Fine Arts Council, as discussed below.

3. The provisions of the Act would not prohibit Edward Miller and Lolly Kupec from providing services to the Tahoe City Public Utility District regarding activities, programs, or plans which are not related to the performing arts unless such activities, programs, or plans would have a foreseeable and material financial effect on Wild West Graphics and Communications or its clients.
